skill.name: log-analysis
skill.description: Analyze application logs to find errors, performance issues, traffic patterns, and security anomalies. Use it for debugging, monitoring reviews, and incident investigation across common text and access log formats.
skill.summary: Find errors, slow requests, suspicious activity, and incident clues in application logs.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell command blocks, hardcoded URLs, a private IP example, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no malicious intent, prompt injection, destructive commands, or data exfiltration. The skill is publishable with a warning because it guides users to run read-only shell pipelines on potentially sensitive logs.

medium_findings.title: Read-only shell command examples require operator control
medium_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE for external command guidance: the skill includes bash examples using grep, tail, awk, cut, sort, uniq, and head. These are legitimate log-analysis commands and no destructive command or outbound transfer was found, but adapting them to untrusted paths or sensitive logs can expose data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are directly present in fenced bash blocks. Their intent is legitimate and read-only, so the confidence is high for the pattern but not for malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Log analysis can expose sensitive data
medium_findings.description: Semantic review found that the skill instructs users to analyze application and access logs, which can contain tokens, passwords, user identifiers, and IP addresses. The skill requires masking sensitive information and prohibits external exposure, reducing but not removing the handling risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly discusses masking passwords and tokens, which confirms that sensitive log content is in scope. No evidence of intentional exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation-only network references
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for network behavior: the hardcoded URLs are reference links to grep, awk, and log-analysis documentation. No command or instruction sends log data to these URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear only in a References section. There is no curl, wget, fetch, or upload instruction tied to them.

low_findings.title: Benign private IP address example
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for hardcoded IP risk: 192.168.1.100 is used as an example search term for local access-log analysis. It is not an external endpoint and is not used for network communication.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The IP appears inside a grep example for finding log activity. The address is private RFC1918 space and no connection is attempted.

low_findings.title: Weak cryptography detections are text false positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE for weak cryptography: review found no cryptographic operation, hash function use, or encryption guidance at the flagged lines. The matches appear to come from ordinary prose and headings, not from security code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain the skill description and an HTTP error-code heading. No weak algorithm name is used as a crypto primitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: External command examples in markdown
dangerous_patterns.description: The static external-command detections are confirmed as markdown examples, not embedded executable code. Commands are read-only log inspection pipelines, but they should be reviewed before execution on sensitive or very large logs.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The fenced command blocks and command references are visible in the skill file. They do not show destructive flags, shell downloads, or outbound network transfer.

content.user_title: Analyze Logs for Errors and Incidents
content.value_statement: Log files are hard to review quickly during debugging, monitoring, or incidents. This skill guides structured searches for errors, slow requests, traffic spikes, and suspicious access patterns.
actual_capabilities: Find ERROR, exception, failure, and HTTP status patterns in text logs.
Group errors by timestamp, source IP, endpoint, or response code.
Identify slow requests and high-traffic endpoints in access logs.
Search for common suspicious patterns such as SQL injection, XSS, traversal, and login abuse.
Produce a concise incident-style report with summary, counts, tables, and recommended actions.
limitations: It provides analysis guidance and command patterns, not a complete log ingestion platform.
It does not parse every custom or binary log format automatically.
It depends on access to relevant local log files and a clear time window.
Sensitive values must be masked before sharing output outside the trusted environment.

use_cases.title: Debug production errors
use_cases.description: Identify recent failures, group repeated errors, and summarize likely root causes from application logs.

use_cases.title: Review service performance
use_cases.description: Find slow requests, busy endpoints, and traffic changes in web server access logs.

use_cases.title: Investigate suspicious activity
use_cases.description: Search access logs for injection attempts, traversal patterns, login abuse, and abnormal IP activity.

prompt_templates.title: Find recent errors
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a quick error scan.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the application logs for the last hour. Summarize error types, counts, timestamps, and likely causes. Mask secrets in any examples.

prompt_templates.title: Compare error patterns
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate debugging across a defined window.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these logs between START_TIME and END_TIME. Group repeated errors, show when each first and last appeared, and recommend next checks.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate slow requests
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the access logs for slow requests and high-volume endpoints. Include response-time outliers, affected paths, and probable bottlenecks.

prompt_templates.title: Run incident triage
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform an incident-focused log review for this outage window. Correlate errors, traffic spikes, suspicious access patterns, and provide prioritized actions.

output_examples.input: Application log review for a failed deployment window.
output_examples.output: A report with the analysis window, total lines reviewed, top error groups, first and last occurrence times, likely deployment links, and recommended rollback or fix checks.

output_examples.input: Web access log review for slow responses.
output_examples.output: A performance summary listing the slowest endpoints, repeated latency spikes, affected status codes, busiest source ranges, and targeted optimization actions.

output_examples.input: Security review of login and access logs.
output_examples.output: A triage summary showing unusual login volume, suspicious request patterns, repeated source IPs, affected endpoints, and containment recommendations.

best_practices: Define the exact time window, service name, and log files before analysis.
Mask passwords, tokens, session identifiers, and personal data before sharing results.
Validate high-impact conclusions against surrounding log context and deployment history.
anti_patterns: Running broad searches across unrelated logs without a time window or incident question.
Pasting raw logs that contain credentials, tokens, or customer personal data.
Treating command output counts as root cause without reviewing nearby context.

faq.question: What log formats does this skill support?
faq.answer: It supports common text logs, web access logs, application logs, and JSON-style logs when fields are readable.

faq.question: Can it analyze Nginx and Apache access logs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes patterns for HTTP status codes, source IP counts, endpoints, and response-time review.

faq.question: Does it modify log files?
faq.answer: No. The skill instructs read-only analysis and explicitly prohibits modifying log files.

faq.question: How should sensitive data be handled?
faq.answer: Mask passwords, tokens, session identifiers, and personal data before sharing any excerpts or summaries.

faq.question: Can it find security attacks in logs?
faq.answer: It can search for common indicators such as injection attempts, XSS strings, traversal paths, and login abuse.

faq.question: Does it replace a monitoring platform?
faq.answer: No. It helps with focused investigation, but it does not provide storage, alerting, dashboards, or long-term indexing.
